content: Note to reviewers: The clash is complete! Please ignore the conversations below as I have already taken care of any suggestions. If you do end up enjoying this reverse puzzle and think this would make a good clash of code, then please do not hesitate to approve. Thank you!

Cases
* While the ordering itself isn't critical for this clash, it's still preferable to group similar cases together. Currently (T = Test, V = Validator):

  - w < h: T2, T3, T4, V6
  - w = h: T1, T5, T6
  - w > h: V1, V2, V3, V4, V5

  Changes to make:
  - Group the w < h cases into two pairs
  - Group the w > h cases (excluding V5) into two pairs
  - Group the w = h cases (excluding T5) into one pair
  - Keep the value of g similar in each pair, including one pair where g = 0, and assign distinct ranges of g to the remaining pairs (e.g. 1 to 3, 4 to 6, and so on).

* Consider adding a pair of cases for each of the scenario below (i.e. 3 pairs in total):

  - w = 1 and h > 1.

  - h = 1 and w > 1.

  - w = h = 1 with different values of g.

Thanks.

What is [[w]] and [[h]] meant to represent?
It looks like [[w]] is the number of vertical separators ({{|}} and {{+}} on intersections), while [[h]] is the number of whitespaces, sections created by the horizontal separators.
From the example:
`o | | | o` - four spaces, three vertical separators; [[w]] is 3
```
 o
 
-
 
-
 
o
```
- three spaces, two horizontal separators; [[h]] is also 3

And while it would be quite simple to deduce this from test cases, did you indent to include this inconsistency?

Also, it seems that this CoC is only for Reverse mode at the moment. Do you plan to make it Fastest/Shortest as well?

Hi @AdSm!

Sorry about the confusing clash and inconsistencies. I had a really wild day yesterday, so I messed up a lot. Anyways, I've given up on making this into a fastest/shortest clash and will just make this Reverse only. Additionally, the width [[w]] and height [[h]] should make more sense now. Could you take a look at this puzzle again and tell me what you think? Thank you!

Hi @5DN1L!

Not sure if I did what you asked correctly, but here are the changes I made to the cases: (T = Test, V = Validator)
T1/V1: w < h
T2/V2: w > h
T3/V3: w = h
T4/V4: g is a really large value and w = h in those cases
T5/V5: g = 1 and w = h in those cases
T6/V6: g = 0 and w > h in one of them and w < h in the other
T7/V7: w = h = 1
T8/V8: h = 1 and w > 1
T9/V9: w = 1 and h > 1
T10/V10: really large tennis rackets
You got it 💯, thanks!

One more thing; today, I just tried out my clash in the IDE, and sometimes, this appeared instead of the tennis racket pattern: ?GREEN?Success?GREEN?

Is this normal and okay for clashes like this, or could this become a problem for other languages (I still got 100% in Python)
Would you share a screenshot somewhere or send it to me via private message on the forum?

Getting the screenshot to you is a bit tricky for me currently, but meanwhile, I think you could paste my solution into the IDE, run it, and you might be able to see the same thing.

import sys
import math

# Initialization
w, h, g = map(int, input().split())

# Racket Grid
print("o"*(w*2+1))
for y in range(h):
  print(end="o ")
  for x in range(w-1):
    print(end="| ")
  print(end="o")
  print()
  if y!=h-1:
    print(end="o-")
    for x in range(w-1):
      print(end="+-")
    print(end="o")
    print()
print("o"*(w*2+1))

# Grip
for i in range(g):
  if i==g-1:
    print(" "*(w-1)+"|_|")
  else:
    print(" "*(w-1)+"| |")

OK. It seems quite random. I clicked on a case a few times, and the weird message only occurred some of the time. I guess it won't affect scoring, but I'm not sure.

Would you like me to post a message in CG's Discord channel tomorrow morning (it's currently night in Europe), or would you prefer to do it yourself? Either works for me.

If it's a big enough issue, then you should do it since I don't have Discord and can't even use it. Since it doesn't affect scoring though, I wouldn't stress over it. I played a clash just now on CoC, and I got the same message while running the test cases.

Agreed; it shouldn't be a major issue. And it affects not only this clash but other clashes (or perhaps puzzles) as well.

I'll report it to CG tomorrow and won't stress about it 😆 Thanks for flagging it, though!

Sorry about the interruption. I know you said you would report this anyways, but this time, you should treat it as a major issue. Sometimes, this appears when my code is incorrect, and it's been getting annoying lately as I'd have to rerun the test case to see if I really got it correct or not. It's happened to nearly every clash I've played today.

And this can be dangerous as if the player accidentally missed an edge case but the edge case got glitched, then the player might submit the code unaware of his/her error, and that would annoy anyone.

> this appears when my code is incorrect

Do you mean "Success" appears when your code is incorrect?
Also, may I know which browser you're using?

You know how the program would have the "Found" and "Expected" error if you output the wrong values, but in my case, the output sometimes just says ?GREEN?Success?GREEN? and makes the test case border a green color, and when you re-run the test case, you finally see the error.

I considered sharing your message on Discord, but I think it would be more helpful if you could reply directly to this forum post instead:

https://forum.codingame.com/t/text-disappears-in-ide-editing-impossible/208108/11

Please include the details you shared with me, along with the browser you're using. The CG team has already responded to that thread earlier today, so they're likely to see your reply there, and it'll make your report easier for them to track and act on. Thanks!
Same  'GREEN?..' issue here, however it is not specific to this contribution, I have started seeing this about 10 days ago.

Yeah, now it is consistent and easier to understand. And as you've made it reverse only, you don't have to worry about explaining the racket grid.

Maybe I would change the first test to have all values distinct, to allow players to take only a quick look (once they know the pattern) at it and know which value corresponds to what. But they'd know that from the second test anyway, so it doesn't really matter that much.

Thanks for the feedback @AdSm! Glad to know that it's better for you now.

Also, I changed the value of [[g]] for Test 1 so that the values are distinct. Hopefully that helps :)
